title: “‘A being, a being,’ venerable sir, is said. To what extent, venerable sir, is one called a being?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving…
content: “‘A being, a being,’ venerable sir, is said. To what extent, venerable sir, is one called a being?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving there is for form — entanglement there, immersion there — thus one is called a being. For feeling … perception … formations … for consciousness — entanglement there, immersion there — thus one is called a being.”

title: “Monks, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end. What…
content: “Monks, these ten perceptions, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end. What ten? The perception of foulness, the perception of death, the perception of disgust with food, the perception of non-delight in the entire world, the perception of impermanence, the perception of suffering in impermanence, the perception of not-self in suffering, the perception of abandoning, the perception of dispassion, the perception of cessation. These ten perceptions, monks, when developed and cultivated, are of great fruit, great benefit, merging with the deathless, having the deathless as their end.”

title: “Monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the fool is generated. That ignorance the fool has not abandoned. That craving the…
content: “Monks, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, this body of the fool is generated. That ignorance the fool has not abandoned. That craving the fool has not destroyed. Why is that? Monks, the fool does not live the holy life for the complete destruction of suffering. Therefore, with the breakup of the body, after death, the fool goes to another body. Having gone to another body, he is not freed from birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair. I say that he is not freed from suffering.”

title: - “Venerable Sāriputta, what things should a virtuous monk attend to wisely?” - “Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to the five aggregates…
content: - “Venerable Sāriputta, what things should a virtuous monk attend to wisely?” - “Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to the five aggregates of clinging as impermanent, as suffering, as a disease, as a boil, as a dart, as misery, as an affliction, as alien, as disintegrating, as empty, as not-self. What five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling aggregate of clinging, the perception aggregate of clinging, the formation aggregate of clinging, the consciousness aggregate of clinging. Venerable Koṭṭhika, a virtuous monk should attend wisely to these five aggregates of clinging as impermanent … as not-self.”

title: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The…
content: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The form of a man, monks, obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman.”

title: “Again, the five kinds of kings Are present in the world — Learn to subdue them, Always keeping mindfulness. Toward forms and sounds, Toward tastes…
content:
“Again, the five kinds of kings
Are present in the world —
Learn to subdue them,
Always keeping mindfulness.
Toward forms and sounds,
Toward tastes and smells,
And toward contacts —
Subdue desire and lust.

Subdue desire
For these things.
The mindful monk,
With mind well liberated,
Then, at the right time,
Rightly reflecting on the Dhamma,
With unified mind,
He should destroy darkness.” So spoke the Blessed One.

title: Elder Raṭṭhapāla: Behold this decorated image — A heap of wounds, a collection of sores, Diseased, full of lustful thoughts, Nothing stable or permanent here.…
content:
Elder Raṭṭhapāla:

Behold this decorated image —
A heap of wounds, a collection of sores,
Diseased, full of lustful thoughts,
Nothing stable or permanent here.

Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings —
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

The feet are painted with lac and dye,
The face is smeared with powder and paste —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hair is curled in eight rings,
The eyes are rubbed with collyrium and rouge —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

Like a newly painted medicine box,
This foul body is adorned —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hunter set his trap and snare —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to mourn.

The hunter’s trap is cut —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to grieve.

title: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by…
content: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving. Monks, suppose a dog was tied by a leash to a strong post or pillar. If it walks, it walks close to that post or pillar. If it stands, it stands close to that post or pillar. If it sits, it sits close to that post or pillar. If it lies down, it lies down close to that post or pillar. So too, monks, an untaught ordinary person regards form as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ If he walks, he walks close to these five aggregates of clinging. If he stands, he stands close to them. If he sits, he sits close to them. If he lies down, he lies close to them. Therefore, monks, one should constantly reflect on one’s own mind thus: ‘For a long time, this mind has been defiled by greed, hatred, and delusion. Through the defilement of the mind, beings are defiled. Through the purification of the mind, beings are purified.’”

title: Elder Mahā-Moggallāna: With hut whose roof is made of bones, Sewn together with sinews and flesh — Foul, full of stench! Carrying other people’s limbs…
content:
Elder Mahā-Moggallāna:

With hut whose roof is made of bones,
Sewn together with sinews and flesh —
Foul, full of stench!
Carrying other people’s limbs
As my own body!

The container full of feces,
Covered over with skin —
O female demon!
With your breasts like boils —
Your body has nine streams,
Always flowing.

Your body’s nine streams,
Foul, creating bondage —
The monk avoids that body
As a clean person avoids feces.

If others knew you
As I know you,
They would avoid you
As one avoids feces in the rain.

It is truly so, O Great Hero,
As the ascetic said:
Here, some are stuck,
Like an old ox in the mud.

One who thinks he can dye
The sky with turmeric,
Or with any other color —
That one only fails.

My mind is like that sky —
Well concentrated internally —
Do not bring evil thoughts to it,
Like a bird falling into a pile of fire.

title: “Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing…
content: “Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing others to lay her down. What do you think, monks? Has her former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Thus, monks, is the peril in the case of forms.”
